metadata.summary: Block releases on license violations via an automated allowlist scan; emit a signed SPDX/CycloneDX SBOM on every release for EU CRA and EO 14028 compliance.

content.title: License Compliance as a Build-Blocking Gate
content.summary: Every release pipeline runs an automated license scan against an explicit allowlist. Free OSS-shipped projects use GitHub's `licensee` for source detection; commercial SaaS or shipped binaries use FOSSA (or Black Duck / ScanCode) to produce an SPDX/CycloneDX SBOM and a deny/flag/approve decision per dependency. Copyleft licenses (GPL, AGPL, SSPL) introduced into proprietary builds block the pipeline; the agent regenerates the `NOTICE` / `THIRD_PARTY.md` attribution file on every dependency change. License scanning runs on PR, not just on release.

why.p: License violations are legal liability, not just hygiene. AGPL pulled into a SaaS triggers a source-disclosure obligation across the entire stack; HashiCorp's BSL switch demonstrated how a single dep license change can invalidate a downstream product. Manual review does not scale once an AI agent is autonomously adding dependencies. Continuous license scanning produces a "living SBOM" required by the EU Cyber Resilience Act (mandatory September 2026 reporting, full December 2027) and US Executive Order 14028; FOSSA reports 99.8% accuracy across 17+ languages and 20+ build systems, which closes the gap human review left open.

list.item: Any product distributed externally — SaaS, downloadable, OSS published — where license obligations attach.
Enterprise procurement contexts where customers ask for an SBOM and a license attestation.
Repos where AI agents add dependencies autonomously; the agent is the new "developer who didn't read the license".
Products subject to EU CRA (anything sold in the EU after Sep 2026) or US federal procurement (EO 14028 SBOM mandate).
Internal-only tools that never leave the org boundary — no redistribution, no obligation.
Hobby projects with zero compliance bar (still good hygiene if free; skip if it slows you down).
Vendored read-only mirrors — license obligations follow the upstream redistribution, not the mirror.
Pure data repos with no compiled artifact — apply data-license review instead.

section.name: allowlist-and-block
section.p: Pin a per-product license allowlist, block copyleft contamination, and force the agent to escalate any non-allowlist license to a human.

License risk depends on distribution. SaaS-only products tolerate Apache/MIT/BSD/MPL but cannot tolerate AGPL. On-prem distributed binaries also forbid GPL except where the build can comply. The policy is encoded as data, not as judgement, so an agent can apply it without ambiguity.

rule.statement: Every repo MUST commit a `license-policy.yml` (or equivalent FOSSA / Black Duck policy) listing `allow`, `flag`, and `deny` SPDX identifiers per build target. The CI MUST fail when any dependency resolves to a `deny` license OR introduces a license not in `allow` / `flag`. For proprietary SaaS the deny list MUST include at minimum `GPL-2.0-only`, `GPL-3.0-only`, `AGPL-3.0-only`, `SSPL-1.0`. The agent MUST refuse to add a dependency whose declared license is not on the allowlist; if uncertain, escalate to a human, never silently accept. On every dependency change the agent MUST regenerate `NOTICE` / `THIRD_PARTY.md` from the lockfile, not by hand.
rule.rationale: Encoding the policy as machine-readable data turns license review from a once-a-quarter human task into a per-PR build gate; the agent gets a deterministic boundary instead of having to "decide" what is acceptable.

example.title: License policy for SaaS target
example.code: # license-policy.yml target: saas allow: [MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-2-Clause, BSD-3-Clause, ISC, MPL-2.0] flag: [LGPL-2.1-only, LGPL-3.0-only, EPL-2.0] # need legal review deny: [GPL-2.0-only, GPL-3.0-only, AGPL-3.0-only, SSPL-1.0, BUSL-1.1] unknown: deny
example.note: `unknown: deny` forces the agent to escalate when license metadata is missing.

example.title: Disabled license checking
example.code: # Effective policy: "anything goes if tests pass" license-check: false
example.note: No policy means the agent accepts AGPL into a SaaS, triggering source-disclosure obligation across the stack.

antipattern.description: Adding a dep with an unknown license and writing a comment "license to be reviewed later" so the build can move.
antipattern.reason: The "later" review never happens; the obligation propagates with every release until a customer audit surfaces it.

section.name: sbom-as-release-artifact
section.p: Emit an SPDX or CycloneDX SBOM with every release, sign it, and treat it as a first-class artifact attached to the tag.

EU CRA mandatory reporting (Sep 2026) and US EO 14028 require a machine-readable SBOM per release in SPDX or CycloneDX. The SBOM is a build artifact, not a PDF: regenerated from the locked dependency graph, signed with cosign, attached to the release.

rule.statement: Every tagged release MUST produce a CycloneDX 1.6 OR SPDX 2.3 SBOM regenerated from the lockfile (`pnpm-lock.yaml`, `uv.lock`, `Cargo.lock`, `go.sum`, `pom.xml`, etc.). The SBOM MUST be signed with `cosign attest` (keyless OIDC or KMS) and attached to the GitHub Release / artifact registry. The SBOM MUST be regenerated — NEVER hand-edited. The CI MUST fail the release if the SBOM is missing or its signature does not verify. Older SBOMs MUST be retained for the regulatory window (EU CRA: 10 years from end-of-support).
rule.rationale: A signed SBOM is the only evidence an auditor can trust; a hand-edited or unsigned SBOM is unfalsifiable and provides no compliance value when a CVE is reported against a transitive dep.

example.title: CI workflow generating and signing SBOM
example.code: # .github/workflows/release.yml (excerpt) - name: Generate SBOM run: | cyclonedx-py --format json --output sbom.cdx.json - name: Sign SBOM run: | cosign attest --predicate sbom.cdx.json \ --type cyclonedx ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.ref_name }} - name: Attach to release uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with: files: sbom.cdx.json
example.note: Generated, signed with keyless OIDC, attached to the release tag.

example.title: Static PDF SBOM
example.code: docs/sbom-2024-q4.pdf # last updated 14 months ago, no signature
example.note: Static PDF, manually maintained, drifts immediately after the next dep bump.

antipattern.description: Generating the SBOM only on quarterly compliance review instead of every release.
antipattern.reason: Between reviews the dep graph shifts; the SBOM no longer matches the deployed artifact, and CVE reachability claims cannot be defended.

template.purpose: Allow / flag / deny policy consumed by FOSSA / scancode-toolkit / `pip-licenses`.
GitHub Actions workflow running licensee + a CycloneDX SBOM emitter on every PR.
